subject: Mathematics
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Math 8 Complete Coverage Add-On
grade_subject_fit: Grade 8 Math add-on for the Kamloops planter project. Students use real garden measurements, plant data, and material decisions to practise core BC Math 8 skills with high scaffolding and reduced question load.
unit_context: Students already have planter boxes built or being built. Herbs are planted in the main planter, and fast-grow seed pucks are being observed before transplanting. Each module uses daily garden interaction: measuring, watering, recording growth, comparing materials, or making a design recommendation.
assignment_purpose: Patch Math 8 coverage gaps through one realistic garden add-on package rather than disconnected worksheets.
final_evidence_target: Students create a short garden recommendation using at least one measurement calculation, one graph or data summary, one budget/cost comparison, and one design check.
student_task_flow: Use one visible math tool or formula.
Record a short calculation or table.
Explain one decision using numbers from the planter project.
success_criteria: I can choose the math tool that fits the garden problem.
I can show my calculation steps with units.
I can use garden data to support a recommendation.

supports_scaffolds.label: Reduced question load
supports_scaffolds.support: Most task sheets use two or three larger questions instead of many small drills.

supports_scaffolds.label: Formula support
supports_scaffolds.support: Formula reminders and sentence frames remain visible on student pages.

supports_scaffolds.label: Differentiated pathway
supports_scaffolds.support: Level A uses completed examples and tables; Level B uses partial frames; Level C adds comparison or justification.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use pre-filled tables, formula boxes, calculators, and one-step garden numbers.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete the main garden calculation and explain one decision using units.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare two options, justify a method, or create a second model using the same data.

checkpoint_release_logic: Teacher checkpoints identify which students need formula review, unit support, or graphing support before the next module.
teacher_implementation_notes: Use the real planter whenever possible. If outdoor access is not possible, use the given numbers on the task sheet.
Keep calculators available. The goal is selecting and setting up the math, not mental arithmetic speed.
Each module can be taught as one larger weekly math block with 10–30 minutes of daily garden data collection.
Do not assign every extension. Choose one path based on student readiness.
likely_misconceptions: Students may confuse area units and volume units.
Students may treat percent change as ordinary subtraction only.
Students may graph plant height without equal intervals on the x-axis.
Students may use the Pythagorean theorem on shapes that are not right triangles.
pacing_shape: Eight weekly modules. Each module has slides, a student task sheet, a short exit ticket, and selected organizers or teacher checkpoints.
assessment_focus: Math reasoning in context: measurement accuracy, unit use, proportional reasoning, equation solving, graph interpretation, data summaries, probability language, and financial decisions.

lesson_overview.title: Plan, Build, Grow — Math 8 Complete Coverage Add-On
lesson_overview.overview: This add-on expands the existing Plan, Build, Grow Math 8 package so the garden project touches the full Grade 8 math year. Students work with planter dimensions, soil and water amounts, plant growth, seed outcomes, material costs, graphing, equations, proportional reasoning, surface area, volume, Pythagorean design checks, and final recommendations.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can Math 8 tools help us make better garden decisions in Kamloops?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Numbers, patterns, and data help us compare choices, check designs, and explain decisions in a real project.

sequence.focus: Measurement, units, perfect squares/cubes, and roots through planter dimensions.
Area, surface area, coverage, and material surfaces.
Volume, soil, watering, and unit conversion.
Ratios, rates, and proportional reasoning with watering and plant spacing.
Percents, germination rate, percent growth, discount, tax, and budget.
Algebraic expressions, two-step equations, input-output tables, and linear relations from growth data.
Pythagorean theorem for diagonal braces, square layout checks, and design accuracy.
Mean, median, mode, range, probability, and final garden recommendation.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Each module gives a Level A support route, a Level B core route, and a Level C extension route using the same garden context.
integrity_checks: Every student-facing task uses garden or planter data.
Teacher-only checkpoints stay separate from student tasks.
No unimplemented output types are declared.
Final response requires numbers, not generic opinion.

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide — Math 8 Garden Complete Coverage Add-On
teacher_guide.big_idea: The planter project can carry the Math 8 year when each math tool is tied to a visible garden decision.
learning_goals: Use perfect squares, perfect cubes, square roots, and cube roots in measurement contexts.
Calculate area, surface area, volume, and unit conversions for planter decisions.
Use ratios, rates, percents, and proportional reasoning with water, growth, seeds, and cost.
Represent plant growth using expressions, equations, input-output tables, linear relations, and graphs.
Use Pythagorean theorem, data analysis, probability, and financial literacy to make a final recommendation.
materials: Existing planter boxes or planter dimension cards
Tape measures, rulers, graph paper, calculators
Material cost cards for soil, lumber, liner, seeds, and tools
Kamloops garden context notes: hot dry days, watering needs, and school budget limits

timing.activity: Garden interaction: observe plants, measure growth, check soil moisture, count seedlings, or record watering.
Launch with slides and one worked example.
Complete the focused task sheet or organizer with teacher support.

teacher_notes: Keep the math visible and concrete. Stand near the planter, use real containers, and model one example before students work.
For support-needs students, reduce numbers but keep the same concept. Do not remove the real context.
Use Level C only when students are ready. The package is designed to be aligned, not identical in quantity.
The final response should be short: numbers, graph/data, cost, and recommendation.
integrity_checks: Student pages should not include teacher checkpoint notes.
Every module should have one practical garden decision attached to it.
Do not assign all optional extensions to the whole class.

days.day_label: Module 1: Measurement, Units, Squares, Cubes, and Roots

content.subtitle: Use planter dimensions to review units, square numbers, cube numbers, and roots.

rows: Area uses square units like cm².
Volume uses cubic units like cm³.
Roots help us work backwards from area or volume to a side length.
Measure or read the planter dimensions.
Identify one square number from a surface area example.
Identify one cube number from a soil-volume example.
Explain why units change from cm to cm² to cm³.

task_sheet.title: Module 1 Task — Garden Measurements and Roots
instructions: Use the planter or the dimension card.
Show units on every number.
Complete Level A, B, or C as assigned.

tasks.label: Part 1: Measure
tasks.prompt: Record one planter dimension set. Length = ___ cm. Width = ___ cm. Height = ___ cm.

tasks.label: Part 2: Squares and cubes
tasks.prompt: Circle the perfect squares: 25, 30, 36, 42. Circle the perfect cubes: 8, 12, 27, 32. Choose one and explain what it could represent in the planter project.

tasks.label: Part 3: Work backward
tasks.prompt: A square garden tile has area 64 cm². Side length = √64 = ___ cm. A cube of soil has volume 125 cm³. Edge length = ∛125 = ___ cm.

embedded_supports: Formula reminder: area is square units; volume is cubic units.
Level A: use the perfect square/cube chart.
success_criteria: I can record measurements with units.
I can identify perfect squares and cubes.
I can use a root to work backward from area or volume.

exit_ticket.title: Module 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Write one example of a square unit and one example of a cubic unit from the planter project.
success_criteria: I named one area unit.
I named one volume unit.

days.day_label: Module 2: Area and Surface Area for Planter Coverage

content.subtitle: Calculate area and surface area to decide how much liner, paint, or cover material is needed.

rows: Area can describe one flat face.
Surface area adds the areas of several faces.
For a planter, coverage decisions depend on which faces need material.
Find bottom area.
Find one side area.
Decide which surfaces need liner or paint.
Do not cover the open top unless the task says so.

task_sheet.title: Module 2 Task — Area and Surface Coverage
instructions: Use A = L × W for each rectangular face.
Label cm² on every area answer.
Add only the faces named in the task.

tasks.label: Part 1: Bottom area
tasks.prompt: Planter base: L = 100 cm, W = 40 cm. Bottom area = ___ × ___ = _____ cm².

tasks.label: Part 2: Side coverage
tasks.prompt: One long side is 100 cm by 30 cm. One short side is 40 cm by 30 cm. Find each area, then explain which pieces need liner.

tasks.label: Part 3: Compare options
tasks.prompt: Level A: choose the larger area from two choices. Level B: add two side areas. Level C: estimate total inside liner area for bottom + four sides.

embedded_supports: Surface area means adding areas of faces.
success_criteria: I can calculate area of a rectangular face.
I can choose which surfaces are part of the problem.
I can add areas to estimate coverage.

graphic_organizer.title: Planter Surface Area Organizer
rows: Bottom
Long side 1
Long side 2
Short side 1
Short side 2
Open top
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use this table to organize which surfaces need material.
success_criteria: I separated the planter into faces.
I recorded area with cm².

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Area and Surface Area
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether students distinguish one-face area from total surface coverage.
look_fors: Uses cm², not cm or cm³
Can explain why the open top is not covered
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move to volume when students can calculate at least one face area and explain which surfaces are included.

days.day_label: Module 3: Volume, Soil, Watering, and Unit Conversion

slides.title: Module 3: Fill and Water the Planter

content.subtitle: Use volume and unit conversion to estimate soil and watering amounts.

rows: Volume = L × W × H.
1000 cm³ is about 1 litre.
Watering amounts are easier to compare in mL or L.
Calculate planter volume.
Convert cm³ to litres.
Estimate soil bags.
Choose a reasonable watering amount for one hot Kamloops day.

task_sheet.title: Module 3 Task — Soil and Water Volume
instructions: Use V = L × W × H.
Convert cm³ to litres by dividing by 1000.
Round soil bags up.

tasks.label: Part 2: Soil bags
tasks.prompt: One soil bag holds 25 L. Bags needed = litres ÷ 25 = ____. Rounded up, buy ____ bags.

tasks.label: Part 3: Watering
tasks.prompt: A plant receives 250 mL of water. Four plants receive ___ mL = ___ L. Explain whether this is reasonable for a hot Kamloops afternoon.

success_criteria: I can calculate volume.
I can convert between cm³, mL, and L in a garden context.
I can explain a watering decision.

exit_ticket.title: Module 3 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Convert 1500 mL to litres. Then explain where mL or L shows up in the garden project.
success_criteria: I converted mL to L.
I connected the unit to watering.

days.day_label: Module 4: Ratios, Rates, and Proportional Reasoning

content.subtitle: Use ratios and rates to compare water, spacing, growth, and planting plans.

rows: A ratio compares two quantities.
A rate compares quantities with different units.
A proportion helps scale a plan up or down.
Compare plants to water volume.
Use a unit rate for water per plant.
Scale a spacing plan for more plants.
Explain which plan is more practical.

task_sheet.title: Module 4 Task — Ratios, Rates, and Plant Spacing
instructions: Write ratios in a clear order.
Use units for rates.
Scale carefully: what you do to one number, do to the other.

tasks.label: Part 1: Water rate
tasks.prompt: Three plants get 750 mL total. Water per plant = 750 ÷ 3 = ___ mL/plant. Six plants would need ___ mL.

tasks.label: Part 2: Plant spacing ratio
tasks.prompt: A spacing plan uses 2 basil plants for every 3 lettuce plants. Draw or write the ratio. If we plant 4 basil plants, how many lettuce plants match the same ratio?

tasks.label: Part 3: Explain
tasks.prompt: Which is easier to manage: equal water for each plant, or different amounts by plant type? Explain using a rate or ratio.

embedded_supports: Level A: use counters or drawings before numbers.
success_criteria: I can write a ratio.
I can find a unit rate.
I can use proportional reasoning to scale a garden plan.

graphic_organizer.title: Water and Plant Ratio Table
rows: 3 plants
6 plants
9 plants
My planter
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use the table to see how water changes when the number of plants changes.
success_criteria: I kept the rate consistent.
I used mL per plant.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Ratios and Rates
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether students can tell the difference between total water and water per plant.
look_fors: Writes a ratio in correct order
Uses per in the unit rate
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move on when students can find one unit rate with support.

days.day_label: Module 5: Percents, Germination, Growth Change, and Budget

content.subtitle: Use percent to describe seed germination, growth change, discount, tax, and garden budget.

rows: Percent means out of 100.
Germination percent compares sprouted seeds to total seeds.
Discount and tax change the final cost.
Find germination percent.
Find percent growth from plant height data.
Apply discount and tax to a garden purchase.
Stay inside the budget.

task_sheet.title: Module 5 Task — Percent in the Garden
instructions: Use percent = part ÷ whole × 100.
Show one calculation.
Use money notation for costs.

tasks.label: Part 1: Germination percent
tasks.prompt: 18 out of 24 seeds sprouted. Percent sprouted = 18 ÷ 24 × 100 = ____%. Level A: use calculator with teacher. Level C: compare to 20 out of 25 seeds.

tasks.label: Part 2: Growth percent
tasks.prompt: A basil plant grew from 8 cm to 10 cm. Change = ___ cm. Percent change = change ÷ original × 100 = ____%. Explain what this means.

tasks.label: Part 3: Budget
tasks.prompt: A soil bag costs $8.00. It is 25% off, then 12% tax is added. Estimate the final cost and decide if 4 bags fit a $30 budget.

embedded_supports: Tax in BC example: use 12% when your teacher asks for combined tax practice.
success_criteria: I can calculate a percent from garden data.
I can use percent to describe change.
I can estimate discount, tax, and budget impact.

exit_ticket.title: Module 5 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: If 15 out of 20 seeds sprout, what percent sprouted? Show the calculation or explain your strategy.
success_criteria: I used part and whole correctly.
I wrote the answer as a percent.

days.day_label: Module 6: Algebra, Equations, Linear Relations, and Graphing

content.subtitle: Use expressions, equations, tables, and graphs to model plant growth over time.

rows: An expression can describe a repeated pattern.
A two-step equation can help find an unknown day or height.
A table and graph show how height changes over time.
Use a starting height and daily growth rate.
Complete an input-output table.
Graph the ordered pairs.
Use the model to predict a future plant height.

task_sheet.title: Module 6 Task — Growth Rules and Graphs
instructions: Use the growth rule given by your teacher.
Complete the table before graphing.
Label both axes.

tasks.label: Part 1: Expression
tasks.prompt: A plant starts at 6 cm and grows 2 cm per day. Expression: 6 + 2d. Find the height when d = 0, 1, 2, 3.

tasks.label: Part 2: Two-step equation
tasks.prompt: Solve: 6 + 2d = 14. Step 1: subtract 6. Step 2: divide by 2. d = ___. What does d mean in the garden?

tasks.label: Part 3: Graph
tasks.prompt: Graph your table. x-axis = days. y-axis = height in cm. Then write one sentence describing the trend.

embedded_supports: Sentence frame: The graph shows ___ because ___.
success_criteria: I can substitute values into an expression.
I can solve a two-step equation with support.
I can make and describe a growth graph.

graphic_organizer.title: Plant Growth Input-Output Table
graphic_organizer.prompt: Use the expression to complete each output before graphing.
success_criteria: I completed the table first.
I used ordered pairs correctly.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Algebra and Graphing
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether students understand d as days and output as height, not two unrelated numbers.
look_fors: Solves a two-step equation with inverse operations
Labels axes with days and cm
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Move to Pythagorean theorem when students can complete a small input-output table.

days.day_label: Module 7: Pythagorean Theorem and Design Checks

content.subtitle: Use the Pythagorean theorem to check diagonal braces and right-angle planter designs.

rows: Pythagorean theorem works with right triangles.
a² + b² = c²
In a rectangular planter, the diagonal across a corner can be checked with this relationship.
Identify the two legs of the right triangle.
Square each leg.
Add the squares.
Take the square root to estimate the diagonal brace length.

task_sheet.title: Module 7 Task — Pythagorean Design Check
instructions: Use the theorem only for right triangles.
Show each step.
Round to the nearest centimetre if needed.

tasks.label: Part 1: Diagonal brace
tasks.prompt: A rectangular frame corner has legs 30 cm and 40 cm. a² + b² = c². 30² + 40² = c². 900 + 1600 = c². c = √2500 = ___ cm.

tasks.label: Part 2: Planter layout check
tasks.prompt: A garden bed has sides 60 cm and 80 cm. Estimate the diagonal. Use the same steps as Part 1.

tasks.label: Part 3: Explain
tasks.prompt: Why might a builder measure diagonals when checking whether a rectangular planter is square?

embedded_supports: Level A: use the 3-4-5 triangle pattern.
success_criteria: I can identify when a right triangle is present.
I can use a² + b² = c² with support.
I can explain why diagonal checks help builders.

exit_ticket.title: Module 7 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: A right triangle has legs 6 cm and 8 cm. Use 6² + 8² = c² to find the diagonal length.
success_criteria: I squared both legs.
I found the square root at the end.

days.day_label: Module 8: Data Analysis, Probability, and Final Recommendation

content.subtitle: Use plant data, probability, and cost information to make a final garden recommendation.

rows: Mean, median, mode, and range summarize data.
Probability describes how likely an outcome is.
A recommendation is stronger when it uses numbers from the project.
Summarize plant growth data.
Describe seed outcome probability.
Compare one cost or budget choice.
Recommend the best next step for the garden.

task_sheet.title: Module 8 Task — Data and Probability Summary
instructions: Use your real class data if available.
If data is missing, use the sample data set.
Choose one final recommendation and support it with numbers.

tasks.label: Part 1: Data summary
tasks.prompt: Plant heights after one week: 6 cm, 8 cm, 8 cm, 10 cm, 13 cm. Find the mean, median, mode, and range.

tasks.label: Part 2: Probability
tasks.prompt: In one tray, 18 of 24 seeds sprouted. Probability of sprouting = 18/24. Write this as a fraction and a percent if you can.

tasks.label: Part 3: Cost comparison
tasks.prompt: Option A costs $36 and supports 12 plants. Option B costs $45 and supports 18 plants. Find cost per plant for each and choose the better value.

embedded_supports: Cost per plant = total cost ÷ number of plants
success_criteria: I can summarize plant data.
I can describe probability using garden outcomes.
I can compare cost using unit price or cost per plant.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Garden Math Recommendation
final_response_sheet.prompt: Make a recommendation for the next garden decision. Choose one: watering plan, planter design, soil purchase, seed choice, or planting layout. Use at least three numbers from the project: one measurement or calculation, one data/probability result, and one cost or budget comparison.
planning_reminders: Use numbers from your own task sheets when possible.
Name the math tool you used.
Keep the answer short and clear.

frame_strip: The measurement/calculation that supports this is ___.
paragraph_support.reminder_box: A strong recommendation uses evidence from the garden, not just opinion.

success_criteria: I made one clear recommendation.
I used at least three numbers from the project.
I explained how the numbers support my decision.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Teacher Checkpoint — Final Math Recommendation
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Check whether final recommendations include measurement, data/probability, and cost evidence.
look_fors: Does not write only personal preference
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Ready to share when the recommendation includes measurement, data/probability, and cost evidence.
